request.question: What Australian academic, author and feminist, was a panelist on the Australian comedy talk show Salam Cafe?

title: Andy Cowan
The Dream with Roy and HG
Roy and HG's Russian Revolution
Susan Carland
The Ice Dream with Roy and HG
Hazel Treweek
Salam Cafe
Rove LA
The Glass House (2001 TV series)
In Siberia Tonight
sentences: Andy Cowan (born Andrew Glenn Cowan) is an American writer and script consultant for television and other media, including two of the five best television series of all time, as compiled in 2012 by ABC News and "People" magazine, and the number two and number eight best written series as voted on by the Writers Guild of America in 2013 (101 Best Written TV Series).
He's hosted the radio comedy talk show, "Up & Down Guys", on KPFK 90.7 FM in Los Angeles.
He has appeared on several television shows, and hosted the hour-long talk show pilot, "Another Talk Show!
with Andy Cowan", featuring Jason Alexander and Sheila Raye Charles.

and was guest commentator on KTTV's "Good Day L.A." He has written humor columns for the "Los Angeles Times" newspaper and magazine, "Salon", special material for "The New Yorker", and several songs, one of which won international song contest nods.
He's a voiceover artist, and sings in jazz clubs throughout Los Angeles.
The Dream with Roy and HG was a sports/comedy talk show, broadcast every night during the Sydney 2000 and Athens 2004 Olympics, presented by Australian comedy duo Roy and HG.
Roy and HG's Russian Revolution was a sports/comedy talk show, broadcast every night during the Sochi 2014 Olympics, presented by Australian comedy duo Roy and HG.
Susan Janet Carland is an Australian academic, author, and feminist, best known for her ongoing media presence speaking on her academic speciality of women in Islam.
The Ice Dream with Roy and HG was a sports/comedy talk show, broadcast every night during the Salt Lake 2002, presented by Australian comedy duo Roy and HG.
Hazel Treweek, OAM, MBE was an Australian academic, teacher and Shakespearean scholar.
She was married to Athanasius "Ath" Treweek, an Australian academic, linguist and World War II cryptographer/codebreaker.
Salam Cafe is an Australian comedy talk show.
Produced by RMITV, and originally airing on Channel 31 from 31 April 2005 under the title Ramadan TV, the show began a revamped ten-week run on the SBS from 7 May 2008.

Hosted by Ahmed Imam and starring various Muslim panellists, including Waleed Aly and Susan Carland, the show presents a light hearted, humorous view on life as a Muslim in Australia through panel discussion and a series of sketches that lampoon the representation of Muslims in Australia and the Islamic way of life.
Rove LA is an Australian television comedy talk show which debuted on Fox8 on 19 September 2011.
The show is hosted by Rove McManus and is set in Los Angeles.
The show is presented in a similar fashion to McManus' previous talk show "Rove", featuring comedy segments and interviews with celebrity guests.
The show was picked up for a two-year run on the Fox8 subscription channel.
The Glass House was a half-hour Australian comedy talk show which screened on the ABC from 2001 to 2006.
In Siberia Tonight is an Australian comedy talk show which aired on SBS from 2005 to 2006.
The program was hosted by comedian Steve Abbott and was themed around his Russian heritage.
The program commonly featured The Kransky Sisters, Indira Naidoo, Steves' mum Evelyn Abbott and Marcello Maio.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What Australian academic, author and feminist, was a panelist on the Australian comedy talk show Salam Cafe?
hop1_docs: Salam Cafe | Salam Cafe is an Australian comedy talk show.  Produced by RMITV, and originally airing on Channel 31 from 31 April 2005 under the title Ramadan TV, the show began a revamped ten-week run on the SBS from 7 May 2008.  Hosted by Ahmed Imam and starring various Muslim panellists, including Waleed Aly and Susan Carland, the show presents a light hearted, humorous view on life as a Muslim in Australia through panel discussion and a series of sketches that lampoon the representation of Muslims in Australia and the Islamic way of life.

Before the Game | Before The Game was an Australian rules football comedy panel television show which aired on Network Ten on 1 March 2003 until 27 September 2013.  The show was hosted by Andrew Maher with regular panelists Mick Molloy, Dave Hughes, Anthony Lehmann and Neroli Meadows.  The format of the show was light-hearted discussion and analysis of Australian Football League (AFL) news and views and included appearances by current players.

Arlene Francis | Arlene Francis (born Arline Francis Kazanjian; October 20, 1907 – May 31, 2001) was an American actress, radio and television talk show host, and game show panelist.  She is known for her long-standing role as a panelist on the television game show " What's My Line? ", on which she regularly appeared for 25 years, from 1950 through the mid-1970s, on both the network and syndicated versions of the show.
Susan Carland | Susan Janet Carland is an Australian academic, author, and feminist, best known for her ongoing media presence speaking on her academic speciality of women in Islam.

Rove LA | Rove LA is an Australian television comedy talk show which debuted on Fox8 on 19 September 2011.  The show is hosted by Rove McManus and is set in Los Angeles.  The show is presented in a similar fashion to McManus' previous talk show "Rove", featuring comedy segments and interviews with celebrity guests.  The show was picked up for a two-year run on the Fox8 subscription channel.
The Eric Bana Show Live | The Eric Bana Show Live was an Australian television comedy and talk show hosted by and named after Eric Bana.

Reihan Salam | Reihan Morshed Salam ( ; born 29 December 1979) is a conservative American political commentator, columnist, and author.  He is the executive editor of "National Review" and a columnist for "Slate", as well as a contributing editor at "National Affairs", an interviewer for VICE, and a fellow at the University of Chicago Institute of Politics.  He has appeared on a number of radio and television shows, including NPR's "Morning Edition, Talk of the Nation, All Things Considered", and "Tell Me More", on HBO's "Real Time with Bill Maher", NBCUniversal's "The Chris Matthews Show", WNYC's "The Brian Lehrer Show", BBC's "Newsnight", ABC's "This Week", CNN's "Fareed Zakaria GPS", Comedy Central's "The Colbert Report", American Public Media's "Marketplace, and Fox News' Tucker Carlson Tonight".
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Salam Cafe page (passage [1]) lists panellists, quoting "including Waleed Aly and Susan Carland" (passage [1]).
  - Susan Carland page (passage [4]) identifies her as "Susan Janet Carland" and as "an Australian academic, author, and feminist" (passage [4]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Salam Cafe lists multiple panellists (e.g., "Waleed Aly and Susan Carland"); only Susan Carland's dedicated page is present in the passages. No alternate person matching "academic, author and feminist" is shown.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "Susan Janet Carland" (as in passage [4]).
  - Missing fact needed: authoritative confirmation that Susan Janet Carland is listed as a Salam Cafe panelist (explicit mention on the Salam Cafe page or on Susan Carland's page linking her to the show).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the full "Salam Cafe" page — check the lede/panelists list and any "Cast" or "Format" sections for an explicit listing of Susan Carland as a panellist. Search keywords: "Salam Cafe panelists Susan Carland".
  2) Open the "Susan Carland" page — check the lede/infobox and sections like "Career", "Media appearances", or "Television" for mention of Salam Cafe. Search keywords: "Susan Carland Salam Cafe".
  3) (If needed) Open "Waleed Aly" page to confirm he is a separate panellist and to ensure no naming/role confusion. Search keywords: "Waleed Aly Salam Cafe".
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and the Salam Cafe–Susan Carland link is explicitly confirmed.
hop2_docs: Salam Cafe | Salam Cafe is an Australian comedy talk show.  Produced by RMITV, and originally airing on Channel 31 from 31 April 2005 under the title Ramadan TV, the show began a revamped ten-week run on the SBS from 7 May 2008.  Hosted by Ahmed Imam and starring various Muslim panellists, including Waleed Aly and Susan Carland, the show presents a light hearted, humorous view on life as a Muslim in Australia through panel discussion and a series of sketches that lampoon the representation of Muslims in Australia and the Islamic way of life.
Susan Carland | Susan Janet Carland is an Australian academic, author, and feminist, best known for her ongoing media presence speaking on her academic speciality of women in Islam.

Carland Cross (TV series) | Carland Cross was an 1996 hardboiled animated television series developed with the collaboration of Belgian, French and Canadian broadcasters.  The series spanned 26 episodes (26 minutes in length) and was based on the comics The Adventures of Carland Cross, by Belgian natives Olivier Grenson and Michel Oleffe.  Carland Cross tells the story of a fictional British private investigator specializing in curious and inexplicable cases.  The series aired late 1996 under the French-language title "Carland Coss" and in other markets, such as Spanish, as Las aventuras de Carland Cross.
Carland Cross (disambiguation) | Carland Cross is a settlement in Cornwall, UK.

Wayne Carlander | Wayne Carlander is a retired American basketball player for the USC Trojans.  A 6'6" power forward, Carlander finished his four-year career with 1,524 points and 767 rebounds in 116 games played.  His point total ranks fifth all-time at the University of Southern California while his rebounds rank eighth.  Carlander lead the Trojans in scoring for three straight seasons (1983–85) and, as a senior, was named the Pac-10 Conference Men's Basketball Player of the Year.  He was also the team captain for his junior and senior years.

Carland Cross | Carland Cross is a location in Cornwall, England, United Kingdom, about six miles (10 kilometres) north of Truro at OS grid reference .  At Carland Cross there are a hamlet, a road junction and a large wind farm.  The road junction is in the civil parish of St Erme, very close to the boundary with St Newlyn East.
John Emmett Carland | John Emmett Carland (December 11, 1853 – November 11, 1922) was a United States federal judge.
response.summary_2: Susan Janet Carland was a panellist on the Australian comedy talk show Salam Cafe. Susan Janet Carland is an Australian academic, author, and feminist.
